title: Core Python — Variables, Strings, Numbers & Print
intro: Before you write tools that scan networks, you must first write simple programs that print results clearly. Master variables, strings, and numbers. The cybersecurity tools you will build this term are made of these building blocks.

title: Core Python — Lists, Loops & Dictionaries
intro: Recon produces lists of hosts and labeled facts. Practice the Python that stores and walks that data — the same constructs behind every tool you will write this term.

title: Core Python — Functions, Modules & Error Handling
intro: Reconnaissance tools are built from reusable functions that import capability from the standard library and handle failure gracefully. Practice the Python that powers every scanner you will write this term.

title: Core Python — requests & JSON
intro: Enumeration turns open ports into actionable intelligence. Practice the Python that speaks HTTP and parses JSON — the same constructs behind every web-enumeration tool you will write this term.

title: Core Python — Bytes, Encoding & Hashing
intro: Cryptography begins with transforming data — encoding it for transport, hashing it for verification, and encrypting it for secrecy. Practice the Python that moves between bytes and text, generates digests, and identifies hash algorithms by their fingerprints.

title: Core Python — Classes & subprocess
intro: Gaining access requires keeping track of session state across multiple attempts. Practice the Python that models that state with a class — then call a mocked system command the way a real tool would.

title: Core Python — Bytes & Payload Assembly
intro: Exploit development demands precision at the byte level. Practice constructing exact payloads with bytes repetition, struct packing, and concatenation — the same constructs that will carry a real exploit in Milestone 3.

title: Core Python — Command-Line Tools with argparse
intro: Professional tools are not one-off scripts — they are polished command-line programs that accept options, report clearly, and chain together. This week you package your week's work into a reusable scanner using `argparse`, the standard library for turning scripts into tools.
